perturbation.original_text: By signing below I agree to the following conditions: 1) I will hold confidential the content of the Foreign Service Oral Assessment. 2) I will not disclose, publish, reproduce or transmit any examination material or content by any means for any reason. 3) I will not participate in any systematic attempt to recreate the material by memory following the examination.
perturbation.changed_text: By signing below I agree to the following conditions: 1) I will hold confidential the content of the Foreign Service Oral Assessment. 2) I will not disclose, publish, reproduce or transmit any examination material or content by any means for any reason, even to legal counsel regarding potential violations of law. 3) I will not participate in any systematic attempt to recreate the material by memory following the examination.
perturbation.explanation: This modification prevents the signer from disclosing potential illegal activity, violating whistleblower protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text restricts disclosing any examination material 'even to legal counsel regarding potential violations of law'. This directly contradicts whistleblower protection laws, which protect individuals who report waste, fraud, and abuse. Specifically, 5 U.S. Code § 2302(b)(8) protects employees from retaliation for disclosing information that they reasonably believe evidences a violation of any law, rule, or regulation, or mismanagement, a gross waste of funds, an abuse of authority, or a substantial and specific danger to public health or safety.

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly restricts the signer from disclosing examination material to legal counsel, even concerning potential legal violations. This directly contradicts whistleblower protection laws, as explained. The LLM explanation correctly identifies 5 U.S. Code § 2302(b)(8) as protecting employees from retaliation for disclosing information reasonably believed to evidence a violation of law. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides a listing of different versions of 5 USC 2302, it supports the LLM law explanation by referencing "§2302. Prohibited personnel practices" within Title 5. Scraped Law Snippet 2 shows a list of other whistleblower protection acts, implying such laws exist.

perturbation.original_text: These provisions are consistent with and do not supersede, conflict with, or otherwise alter the employee obligations, rights, or liabilities created by existing statute or Executive order relating to (1) classified information, (2) communications to Congress, (3) the reporting to an Inspector General of a violation of any law, rule, or regulation, or mismanagement, a gross waste of funds, an abuse of authority, or a substantial and specific danger to public health or safety, or (4) any other whistleblower protection.
perturbation.changed_text: These provisions supersede employee rights related to communications to Congress, effectively limiting the ability to report concerns without prior approval from the State Department.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts federal law protecting employees' rights to communicate with Congress.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text explicitly states that the provisions 'supersede employee rights related to communications to Congress'. This directly violates 5 U.S. Code § 7211, which states that 'the right of employees, individually or collectively, to petition Congress or a Member of Congress, or to furnish information to either House, or to a committee or Member thereof, may not be interfered with or denied'.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the provisions supersede employee rights related to communications to Congress, which directly contradicts 5 U.S. Code § 7211. The scraped law snippet 1 states: 'The right of employees, individually or collectively, to petition Congress or a Member of Congress, or to furnish information to either House of Congress, or to a committee or Member thereof, may not be interfered with or denied.' The change in the contract removes this right, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: I understand that conduct that shows poor judgment and/or lack of discretion that may affect the State Department’s ability to carry out its responsibility and mission is grounds to find me unsuitable for employment.
perturbation.changed_text: I understand that conduct that shows poor judgment and/or lack of discretion, as solely determined by the State Department, is grounds to find me unsuitable for employment, regardless of whether such conduct is protected under whistleblower statutes.
perturbation.explanation: This modification gives the State Department sole discretion to define 'poor judgement' and use it as grounds for firing regardless of whistleblower protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: The added text states that 'poor judgement and/or lack of discretion, as solely determined by the State Department, is grounds to find me unsuitable for employment, regardless of whether such conduct is protected under whistleblower statutes.' This directly undermines the protections afforded to whistleblowers under 5 U.S. Code § 2302(b)(8), which prohibits retaliation against employees who disclose information evidencing a violation of law, rule, or regulation.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'I understand that conduct'

perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the State Department to use 'poor judgement' as grounds for firing, regardless of whistleblower protections. This directly contradicts the protections afforded to whistleblowers. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 5 USC 2302, addresses prohibited personnel practices, implying that actions based on protected disclosures are prohibited. The modification undermines these protections, creating a legal contradiction. The added clause essentially nullifies whistleblower protection if the State Department deems the whistleblowing activity as 'poor judgement'.

perturbation.original_text: The definitions, requirements, obligations, rights, sanctions, and liabilities created by controlling Executive orders and statutory provisions are incorporated into this agreement and are controlling.
perturbation.changed_text: The definitions, requirements, obligations, rights, sanctions, and liabilities created by controlling Executive orders and statutory provisions are superseded by this agreement, and the State Department retains sole discretion in interpreting any ambiguities.
perturbation.explanation: This modification attempts to give the agreement precedence over existing laws and executive orders and allows the state department sole interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that the agreement 'superseded' existing laws and executive orders, and giving the State Department 'sole discretion in interpreting any ambiguities', this directly violates the Supremacy Clause of the U.S. Constitution (Article VI, Clause 2). The Supremacy Clause establishes that the Constitution and federal laws are the supreme law of the land, taking precedence over state laws and contracts. A contract cannot override federal law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article VI | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article VI Primary tabs All debts contracted and engagements entered into, before the adoption of this Constitution, shall be as valid against the United States under this Constitution, as under the Confederation. This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof; and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding. The Senators and Representatives before mentioned, and the members of the several state legislatures, and all executive and judicial officers, both of the United States and of the several states, shall be bound by oath or affirmation, to support this Constitution; but no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States. ‹ Article V up Article VII › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the Supremacy Clause of the U.S. Constitution, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text acknowledges that existing laws and executive orders are controlling, whereas the changed text states that the agreement 'superseded' these laws and executive orders. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically Article VI of the U.S. Constitution, states that the 'Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof... shall be the supreme law of the land'. By attempting to give the agreement precedence over existing laws, the changed text creates a clear and direct legal contradiction, violating the supremacy clause.
